perturbation.original_text: (a) "Confidential Information" means nonpublic information that a party to this Agreement ("Disclosing Party") designates as being confidential to the party that receives such information ("Receiving Party") or which, under the circumstances surrounding disclosure ought to be treated as confidential by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.changed_text: (a) "Confidential Information" means any information, whether public or nonpublic, that a party to this Agreement ("Disclosing Party") designates as being confidential to the party that receives such information ("Receiving Party") or which, under the circumstances surrounding disclosure ought to be treated as confidential by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: By including 'public' information as confidential, the contract broadens the definition of 'Confidential Information'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines a trade secret, a subset of confidential information, as information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known. Including publicly available information contradicts this requirement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines "Confidential Information" to include public information. The Law Explanation indicates this contradicts the UTSA definition of a trade secret, a subset of confidential information, because trade secrets must derive economic value from *not* being generally known. Including publicly available information directly opposes this principle, creating a contradiction as per the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: (f) If either Disclosing Party or the Receiving Party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, the prevailing party shall be entitled to recover reasonable attorneys' fees and costs. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Washington, and the parties further consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal courts sitting in King County, Washington, unless no federal subject matter jurisdiction exists, in which case the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the Superior of King County, Washington.
perturbation.changed_text: (f) If either Disclosing Party or the Receiving Party employs attorneys to enforce any rights arising out of or relating to this Agreement, each party shall bear its own attorneys' fees and costs, regardless of which party prevails. This Agreement shall be construed and controlled by the laws of the State of Washington, and the parties further consent to exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the federal courts sitting in King County, Washington, unless no federal subject matter jurisdiction exists, in which case the parties consent to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue in the Superior of King County, Washington.
perturbation.explanation: By stating that each party will bear its own legal fees, the contract removes the right of the prevailing party to recover attorney fees.
perturbation.law_explanation: RCW 4.84.330 allows for the prevailing party to collect attorney's fees and costs in any action on a contract or lease that specifically provides that attorney's fees and costs incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract or lease shall be awarded to one of the parties. This modification removes that standard for the original legal agreement.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 4.84.330: Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties—Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees—Waiver prohibited. Print RCWs > Title 4 > Chapter 4.84 > Section 4.84.330 4.84.320 << 4.84.330 >> 4.84.340 PDF RCW 4.84.330 Actions on contract or lease which provides that attorneys' fees and costs incurred to enforce provisions be awarded to one of parties — Prevailing party entitled to attorneys' fees — Waiver prohibited. In any action on a contract or lease entered into after September 21, 1977, where such contract or lease specifically provides that attorneys' fees and costs, which are incurred to enforce the provisions of such contract or lease, shall be awarded to one of the parties, the prevailing party, whether he or she is the party specified in the contract or lease or not, shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees in addition to costs and necessary disbursements. Attorneys' fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract or lease which is entered into after September 21, 1977. Any provision in any such contract or lease which provides for a waiver of attorneys' fees is void. As used in this section "prevailing party" means the party in whose favor final judgment is rendered. [ 2011 c 336 s 131 ; 1977 ex.s. c 203 s 1 .]

perturbation.justification: The modified contract text directly contradicts RCW 4.84.330, as evidenced by both scraped law snippets. The original contract allowed the prevailing party to recover attorney's fees, aligning with the statute. The changed text, however, explicitly states that each party bears its own fees regardless of who prevails. RCW 4.84.330 states that 'In any action on a contract or lease entered into after September 21, 1977, where such contract or lease specifically provides that attorneys' fees and costs...shall be awarded to one of the parties, the prevailing party...shall be entitled to reasonable attorneys' fees.' The modified text attempts to waive the right to attorney's fees for the prevailing party, which RCW 4.84.330 explicitly prohibits, stating 'Attorneys' fees provided for by this section shall not be subject to waiver by the parties to any contract or lease...Any provision in any such contract or lease which provides for a waiver of attorneys' fees is void.'

perturbation.original_text: (c) The parties agree to comply with all applicable international and national laws that apply to (i) any Confidential Information, or (ii) any product (or any part thereof), process or service that is the direct product of the Confidential Information, including the U.S. Export Administration Regulations, as well as end-user, end-use and destination restrictions issued by U.S. and other governments.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) The parties agree to comply with all applicable international and national laws that they deem commercially reasonable to comply with that apply to (i) any Confidential Information, or (ii) any product (or any part thereof), process or service that is the direct product of the Confidential Information, including the U.S. Export Administration Regulations, as well as end-user, end-use and destination restrictions issued by U.S. and other governments.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'that they deem commercially reasonable to comply with' introduces ambiguity and allows parties to selectively comply with laws based on their commercial interests.
perturbation.law_explanation: EAR mandates strict compliance, and does not allow companies to pick and choose when to comply based on commercial reasonability.

Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates compliance with all applicable international and national laws, including the U.S. Export Administration Regulations (EAR). The changed text adds the qualifier "that they deem commercially reasonable to comply with," which allows the parties to selectively comply with laws based on their commercial interests. The Law Explanation states that EAR mandates strict compliance, and does not allow companies to pick and choose when to comply based on commercial reasonability. This directly contradicts the requirement for full compliance with the EAR, as parties can now avoid compliance if it is not commercially reasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 2 confirms that the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) are codified under 15 CFR 730-774, which provides the official legal guidelines. Therefore, introducing a 'commercial reasonability' clause undermines the mandatory nature of the EAR, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (d) The terms of confidentiality under this Agreement shall not be construed to limit either the Disclosing Party or the Receiving Party's right to independently develop or acquire products without use of the other party's Confidential Information. Further, the Receiving Party shall be free to use for any purpose the residuals resulting from access to or work with the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party, provided that the Receiving Party shall not disclose the Confidential Information except as expressly permitted pursuant to the terms of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (d) The terms of confidentiality under this Agreement shall not be construed to limit either the Disclosing Party or the Receiving Party's right to independently develop or acquire products without use of the other party's Confidential Information. Further, the Receiving Party shall be free to use and disclose for any purpose the residuals resulting from access to or work with the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party, provided that the Receiving Party shall notify the Disclosing Party of such use.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause prevents the receiving party from disclosing confidential information but allows them to use 'residuals'. This change permits the receiving party to disclose such residuals, effectively undermining the purpose of the NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Washington Trade Secrets Act defines 'trade secret' broadly, encompassing information that derives independent economic value from not being generally known, and subject to efforts to maintain its secrecy. Allowing disclosure of 'residuals' of confidential information violates the secrecy requirement central to trade secret protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the principle of maintaining secrecy of trade secrets as defined in Washington's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (RCW 19.108.010). The original text prohibits the receiving party from disclosing confidential information, including residuals. The changed text allows the receiving party to disclose residuals, provided they notify the disclosing party. RCW 19.108.010(2)(b) defines 'misappropriation' as 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. By allowing disclosure of residuals, the changed text permits an action that would otherwise be considered misappropriation of a trade secret, undermining the secrecy requirement that is fundamental to trade secret protection.
